design_philosophy.core_loop: Select trading routes, negotiate prices across systems, manage fuel/repair costs, accumulate wealth to upgrade ship capabilities, repeat with higher-risk ventures
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Risk vs. reward in illegal trading; survival in anarchic systems; opportunity cost of time spent traveling vs. time spent trading/earning
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become an elite space trader and combat pilot; chart your own course through a procedurally-generated galaxy; build wealth and reputation through any ethical framework you choose
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects arcade flight for uncompromising Newtonian physics; no win condition or plot completion—players must self-author goals; faction rivalry is present but players are free to ignore or exploit it; copy protection integrated as narrative (police verification); illegal goods are profitable but risky, rewarding player calculation over moral guidance

difficulty.continues_system: Permadeath implied (destroyed ship suggests restart); no explicit continues documented
difficulty.checkpointing: Auto-save between jumps; pilot's position and ship state persist across sessions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High—players choose between safe/profitable trade routes in Core worlds vs. dangerous anarchic systems; can toggle autopilot for routine navigation; time acceleration allows skipping tedious travel; combat avoidance is viable through route planning and system selection

progression.upgrade_system: Ship upgrades purchased with accumulated credits; starting with modest Eagle Long Range Fighter and 100 credits; larger cargo holds, better weapons, faster engines imply tiered progression
progression.unlock_mechanism: Wealth-gated (can only afford better ships/weapons as profits accumulate); military rank increases with faction missions (Federation and Empire ranks independent); access to anarchic systems always available but increasingly dangerous
replayability_hooks: No win condition—players set personal goals (e.g., accumulate X credits, achieve Elite combat rating, control trade route)
Faction choice creates alternate playstyles (Federation vs. Empire vs. neutral)
Procedurally generated star systems ensure unique exploration each playthrough
Illegal goods trading offers high-risk/high-reward alternative to safe routes

world_building_techniques: System government types dictate safety and legal trade goods (Core worlds safe, anarchic systems hazardous)
Hyperspace clouds as physical remnants of travel (pirates/assassins can track jumps)
Ship manual as diegetic copy protection (police spot-checks normalize world detail)
Historical inheritance (player is Commander Jameson's grandchild) provides minimal narrative framing

danger_signals: Anarchic systems (Riedquat, Phekda noted as notorious)
environmental_storytelling: Core worlds visibly safer (stronger police presence implied)
Anarchic systems marked by absence of government/safety
Trade goods legality varies by system (reflects local values/laws)
Hyperspace clouds serve as evidence of passage
